item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image could not be sourced and thus is assumed to be non-compliant, and the second image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the road let's them makes them drive forever, age faster and fuck's with thier mind?

Neat.

Too bad no D-Personel could be interviewed afterward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Guess you could really say that for these people, life really is a highway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We can't stop here. This is bat country.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm imagining the look on some passerby's face when one of these pulls up with a skeleton driving and it tips it's hat to them XD +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: bit of a tagging nitpick, this thing clearly altered this guys thought process… is it a cognitohazard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly I found the way the guy was so casual about what had happened to be kinda creepy. You just spent what? 81 years behind the wheel of that car and all you have to say is how astounded you are at the gas prices? Upvote.

item.title: I am very surprised this hasn't been brought up yet
item.content:encoded: Cut the gas, there ain't no brakes on this lost high way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would be funny to see the dumb look on 973's face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is a pretty cool twist, I just felt it was a bit off from the established tone of visceral horror at how fucked up this is.

And personally, that's part of why I adore it. This is something that's profoundly unsettling without having to be pointlessly grimdark. There's far too little of that on the site, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is a pretty cool twist, I just felt it was a bit off from the established tone of visceral horror at how fucked up this is. Definitely still a lot of dread and terror of the concept here (you got my upvote didn't you?), but I guess I just anticipated something more tormenting for those involved, although I suppose that's been done before (no offense to you, but I love SCP-112 and don't think this would measure up if subjected to that kind of comparison).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The people driving the car don't even seem to realize that they're starving and thirsty, their organs are failing, or that they're growing older. They don't even realize that stopping the car will cause all of that to catch up and kill them - they just don't feel like stopping. That's the twist I was going for - less "Why is this happening, please let me die" and more "I can park any time I want to, I just don't feel like it".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eeeeehhhhh, really on the fence here. It's a good concept, but the interview just seems kind of silly. It seemed sinister at first, but the guy seems to have such an unnatural bluntness about not stopping that is obviously intended as creepy, but to me just seems to kill the tone the article built up to that point (images of emaciated, decrepit bodies being forced to drive forever, unable to stop for whatever reason as they slowly die). Nonetheless everything before it is quite good, so it gets my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There were obviously reports of missing travelers, but keep in mind most of the disappearances happened pre-1950, and it was comparatively easier, especially during the Depression, for someone to "head out west" and never be heard from again. It wasn't until the interstate was built and fewer people were driving that stretch that someone (probably the FBI or state police) started to connect the disappearances to that area, and that's when the Foundation would have stepped in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, at least 1% of the cars that drive along that stretch of highway disappear at night, and: 1) over 10,000 cars had to disappear before the Foundation noticed, 2) nobody else noticed before the Foundation did. Seems unlikely to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this all comes together very nicely. It's separate from your typical "disappearing drivers" ghost story (it doesn't happen when they're closely watched, doesn't happen all the time even if they aren't, and they become part of the SCP) and the interview really helps flesh things out. Of course, what squicked me were the fetuses. That's when I knew I was dealing with some heavy shit. Great work on this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: AH, driving SCPs. I get anxiety enough when I attempt to get on the road. These aren't helping. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nicely done. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mr. Bones says, the ride never ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another vote for "was on the fence until I read the logs". Upvoted. And I especially like the bit about aged fetuses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now imagine if Smokey and this met.

RUN FOREVER.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, driving forever's always been a fear of mine. Take your +1 while I go curl up in a corner for a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff right here, upvoted. It is a bit similar to the excellent 973, but as Drewbear mentioned, different enough to stand on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some similar ones (SCP-112, SCP-342 & SCP-973 come to mind), but there aren't any quite like this. The similarites are what made me iffy about this in the first place, but I feel that it's sufficiently different, and sufficiently well done, for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't there an SCP the same as this already?
Assuming for now that there isn't, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any which way you look at it, he gets the upvotes. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I agree with Drewbear. The article really sings when you get to the notes on victims and the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, I'm the opposite. I was iffy about the article all the way until the interview, which clinched it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the feel of the article, but the interview kind of spoils it for me. +1 though, because it's well-written and the concept is classic, but doesn't feel tired. That's hard to accomplish.
